---
title: "Noise Models and Likelihoods"
subtitle: "Chapter 4"
---

[Slides](../slides/week-04-noise-likelihood.html) | [Notebook](../notebooks/week04_noise_likelihood.ipynb) | [Open in Colab](https://colab.research.google.com/github/lnajman/math435-mathematical-imaging/blob/main/notebooks/week04_noise_likelihood.ipynb)

## Learning Goals

By the end of this chapter, you should be able to:

- distinguish deterministic degradation from random noise;
- model additive Gaussian noise;
- model photon-counting Poisson noise;
- compare absolute and relative noise behavior;
- distinguish random noise from model error;
- derive likelihood-based data terms for reconstruction.

## Why Noise Models Matter

In the first chapters we wrote idealized forward models such as blur or Fourier filtering. Real measurements also contain random variation.

A common observation model is

$$
y = f(x) + \eta,
$$

where $x$ is the unknown scene or image, $f$ is the forward imaging process, $y$ is the recorded data, and $\eta$ is noise.

When the forward model is linear, this becomes

$$
y = Ax + \eta.
$$

Linearity is a useful special case, not a universal law of imaging. Saturation, thresholding, occlusion, and many camera pipelines can be nonlinear or irreversible. The likelihood framework still applies if we replace $Ax$ by a general prediction $f(x)$.

This chapter changes the question slightly. Instead of asking only "what image could have produced the data?", we ask "how probable are these data if the image were $x$?" That statistical question is the beginning of data fidelity.

This matters because reconstruction methods are not built from regularization alone. They usually contain two ingredients:

- a data term, which tells us how seriously to take disagreement with the measurements;
- a prior or regularizer, which tells us what kinds of images are plausible when the data are incomplete or unstable.

Noise models are how we derive the first ingredient. Without them, a cost function such as $\|Ax-y\|_2^2$ can look like a purely algebraic habit. With them, the same expression becomes a statement about the measurement process.

## Seeing Noise Before Modeling It

The same clean image can be corrupted in visibly different ways. Additive Gaussian noise has a roughly brightness-independent texture. Photon-counting noise changes with the number of photons reaching each pixel.

::: {.figure}
![](../assets/figures/week04-noise-models.png){fig-alt="Clean image, Gaussian noisy image, low-light Poisson noisy image, and higher-photon Poisson noisy image"}

Different noise models create different visual artifacts.
:::

For a pixel $i$, the recorded value $y_i$ is a random variable. A noise model specifies the probability law of $y_i$ when the unknown true value is $x_i$.

A useful noise model answers:

- what recorded values are likely;
- how large typical errors are;
- whether the variance depends on intensity;
- what cost should penalize disagreement with data.

When you look at the figure, pay attention to two different effects. The Gaussian image has a similar grain in dark and bright regions. The Poisson image is more uneven in low-light regions because the number of photons there is small. The important point is not only that the images look different. The important point is that the same numerical error may have different statistical meanings in different parts of the image.

For example, an error of five gray levels may be large in a dark region and ordinary in a bright region under a photon-counting model. A good data term should know this.

## Gaussian Noise

The simplest model is additive Gaussian noise:

$$
y_i = x_i + \eta_i,
\qquad
\eta_i \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2).
$$

The parameter $\sigma$ is the standard deviation of the noise. Under this model,

$$
\mathbb{E}[y_i\mid x_i] = x_i,
\qquad
\mathrm{Var}(y_i\mid x_i)=\sigma^2.
$$

The variance is constant: it does not depend on $x_i$.

::: {.figure}
![](../assets/figures/week04-noise-histograms.png){fig-alt="Histogram of Gaussian residuals and histograms of Poisson counts for several intensities"}

Gaussian residuals are centered and symmetric, while Poisson counts change shape with intensity.
:::

For one pixel,

$$
p(y_i\mid x_i)
=
\frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}}
\exp\left(
-\frac{(y_i-x_i)^2}{2\sigma^2}
\right).
$$

If pixel errors are independent, then

$$
p(y\mid x)=\prod_i p(y_i\mid x_i).
$$

Taking negative logarithms gives

$$
-\log p(y\mid x)
=
\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_i (y_i-x_i)^2
+ \text{constant}.
$$

Thus Gaussian noise leads naturally to least squares.

## Gaussian Data Fidelity

For direct observation $y=x+\eta$, maximum likelihood gives

$$
\operatorname*{argmin}_x
\frac{1}{2}\|y-x\|_2^2
= y.
$$

For a linear forward model

$$
y = Ax + \eta,
\qquad
\eta\sim\mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2 I),
$$

the Gaussian negative log-likelihood is

$$
\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\|Ax-y\|_2^2.
$$

For a general nonlinear forward model

$$
y = f(x)+\eta,
$$

the corresponding Gaussian data term is

$$
\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\|f(x)-y\|_2^2.
$$

The squared error is therefore not just a convenient algebraic choice. It is the data fidelity term associated with a particular statistical assumption.

## Gaussian Limitations

Gaussian noise is useful and mathematically friendly, but it is not always physically faithful. An additive Gaussian model can produce negative intensities or values above the sensor maximum. In practice we often clip values, but clipping changes the statistical model.

This is a general lesson: a simple model can be useful without being literally complete.

Gaussian models are still common for good reasons. They are often reasonable approximations when many independent small error sources are added together, and they lead to convex quadratic objectives that are easy to analyze and optimize. In this course, Gaussian noise is also the cleanest way to understand the bridge from probability to least squares.

The caution is that "least squares" should not become automatic. Before using it, ask what kind of measurement error is being assumed.

## Weighted Least Squares

The ordinary squared error assumes that each residual has the same variance. If different measurements have different variances, the likelihood leads to a weighted squared error instead.

Suppose

$$
y_i = f_i(x)+\eta_i,
\qquad
\eta_i\sim\mathcal{N}(0,\sigma_i^2),
$$

with independent errors. Then the negative log-likelihood contains

$$
\frac12
\sum_i
\frac{(f_i(x)-y_i)^2}{\sigma_i^2}.
$$

Measurements with smaller variance receive larger weight because they are more reliable under the model. Measurements with larger variance receive smaller weight because larger deviations are less surprising.

This is the main idea behind many data terms: the residual is not judged only by its numerical size. It is judged relative to the uncertainty of the measurement.

## Poisson Noise

Light arrives as photons, and a sensor counts random arrivals during exposure. When photon counting is the dominant source of randomness, a Poisson model is natural.

Let $z_i$ be the photon count at pixel $i$:

$$
z_i \sim \operatorname{Poisson}(\lambda_i).
$$

The Poisson law is

$$
\mathbb{P}(z_i=k\mid \lambda_i)
= e^{-\lambda_i}\frac{\lambda_i^k}{k!}.
$$

For a Poisson random variable,

$$
\mathbb{E}[z_i]=\lambda_i,
\qquad
\mathrm{Var}(z_i)=\lambda_i.
$$

The standard deviation is $\sqrt{\lambda_i}$. Absolute noise increases with brightness, but relative noise decreases:

$$
\frac{\sqrt{\lambda_i}}{\lambda_i}
=
\frac{1}{\sqrt{\lambda_i}}.
$$

This is why low-light imaging is difficult. A pixel receiving 5 photons is much less reliable, relatively speaking, than a pixel receiving 500 photons.

::: {.figure}
![](../assets/figures/week04-snr-curves.png){fig-alt="Gaussian and Poisson signal-to-noise ratio curves"}

Signal-to-noise behavior depends strongly on the noise model.
:::

## From Counts To Intensities

Digital images often store normalized intensities in $[0,1]$. If $\alpha$ is the maximum expected photon count, a simple count model is

$$
z_i \sim \operatorname{Poisson}(\alpha x_i),
\qquad
y_i = z_i/\alpha.
$$

The normalized value $y_i$ is still random. Increasing exposure increases $\alpha$, which improves relative precision when the model is valid.

## Poisson Likelihood

For independent photon counts,

$$
p(z\mid x)
=
\prod_i
e^{-\alpha x_i}
\frac{(\alpha x_i)^{z_i}}{z_i!}.
$$

Ignoring constants independent of $x$,

$$
-\log p(z\mid x)
=
\sum_i
\alpha x_i - z_i\log(\alpha x_i).
$$

For a general positive forward model $f_i(x)>0$,

$$
z_i \sim \operatorname{Poisson}(f_i(x))
$$

and

$$
-\log p(z\mid x)
=
\sum_i f_i(x) - z_i\log(f_i(x))
+ \text{constant}.
$$

This is not a least-squares data term.

The positivity condition is not cosmetic. A Poisson mean must be nonnegative, and the logarithm requires a positive predicted count. In numerical code, this often forces us to constrain the reconstruction or to evaluate $\log(f_i(x)+\epsilon)$ with a very small $\epsilon$. Such details are not just programming details; they are part of making the mathematical model usable.

::: {.figure}
![](../assets/figures/week04-likelihood-curves.png){fig-alt="Gaussian and Poisson negative log-likelihood curves for one pixel"}

Likelihood curves encode how each model penalizes disagreement with the data.
:::

Reading this plot is an important skill. A likelihood curve is steep where the model says a mismatch is unlikely, and flatter where the model is more tolerant. For Gaussian noise with fixed variance, the curve is a symmetric parabola. For Poisson data, the curve is asymmetric because counts cannot be negative and because the variance changes with the mean.

## Gaussian Versus Poisson

| Feature | Gaussian | Poisson |
|---|---|---|
| Data type | continuous intensity | integer counts |
| Variance | constant $\sigma^2$ | equals mean |
| Typical fidelity | squared error | count likelihood |
| Natural for | read noise, aggregate errors | photon-limited imaging |
| Constraint | often unconstrained | positive mean |

The choice of data term should come from the measurement process. A squared error term may be appropriate for Gaussian read noise, but a photon-limited acquisition suggests a Poisson likelihood.

Real cameras may contain several noise sources at once. A simplified pipeline might include photon noise, electronic read noise, dark current, quantization, clipping, compression, and demosaicing. Modeling every part of that pipeline may be unnecessary for a first reconstruction method, but it is useful to know what has been simplified away.

This is one reason image restoration papers often specify both the degradation model and the noise model. "Denoising" by itself is not one problem. Denoising Gaussian noise, photon noise, JPEG artifacts, and sensor artifacts are related but not identical tasks.

## A One-Pixel Likelihood Example

It is useful to pause on a single pixel before returning to full images.

Suppose a sensor records the value

$$
y_i=20.
$$

Under a Gaussian model with fixed standard deviation $\sigma=5$, the candidates $x_i=15$ and $x_i=25$ are treated symmetrically. Both are five units away from the observation. The squared residual is the same:

$$
(15-20)^2=(25-20)^2.
$$

Under a Poisson count model, the comparison is different. If the predicted mean is $\lambda=15$, the observation $20$ is above the mean. If the predicted mean is $\lambda=25$, the observation $20$ is below the mean. These two discrepancies are not judged only by their absolute distance from 20. They are judged by how likely a count of 20 is under each proposed mean.

This is the main reason likelihood matters. A residual is not self-explanatory. It becomes meaningful only after we say what variability the measurement process allows.

For a whole image, the same idea is repeated over many pixels or measurements. The data term adds up many local judgments:

$$
D(f(x),y)
=
\text{total surprise of the observed data if the image were }x.
$$

This is not a formal definition of every possible data term, but it is a good reading habit. A data term measures how surprising the observation would be under a proposed reconstruction and measurement model.

## Noise Versus Model Error

Noise is random variation around a model. Model error means the model itself is wrong or incomplete.

These two failures can look similar in an image, but they have different meanings:

| Issue | Example | Why it matters |
|---|---|---|
| random noise | photon counts fluctuate around the correct mean | repeated measurements may help |
| model error | the assumed blur kernel is wrong | repeated measurements repeat the wrong model |
| missing information | a region was never observed | a prior is needed to choose a completion |
| nonlinear sensor effect | clipping saturates bright pixels | Gaussian residuals may be misleading |

Likelihoods describe random data variation conditional on a model. They do not automatically protect us from a wrong forward model, a wrong noise law, or a missing physical effect.

This distinction becomes important for learned reconstruction. A network may be trained on one degradation model and tested on another. If it performs poorly, the failure may not be "noise" in the usual sense. It may be model mismatch or distribution shift.

## Repeated Measurements

If

$$
y^{(j)} = x + \eta^{(j)},
\qquad
\eta^{(j)}\sim\mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2),
$$

and the noise samples are independent, then the average

$$
\bar{y}=\frac{1}{m}\sum_{j=1}^m y^{(j)}
$$

has noise standard deviation $\sigma/\sqrt{m}$.

::: {.figure}
![](../assets/figures/week04-averaging.png){fig-alt="Clean image and averages of 1, 2, 4, and 16 Gaussian noisy observations"}

Independent random noise can average out.
:::

Averaging cannot recover information that never reached the sensor. If scene content is physically hidden behind an occluder, repeated photographs from the same viewpoint do not reveal the hidden object. This is different from reducing random fluctuations in recorded pixels.

## From Likelihood To Reconstruction

Given data $y$, maximum likelihood estimates $x$ by

$$
\operatorname*{argmax}_x p(y\mid x),
$$

or equivalently

$$
\operatorname*{argmin}_x -\log p(y\mid x).
$$

Maximum likelihood uses the data model, but it is often not enough. In inverse problems, many images may agree almost equally well with noisy data.

A common regularized reconstruction form is

$$
\operatorname*{argmin}_x
D(y,f(x)) + \lambda R(x).
$$

Here $D$ is the data fidelity term from the noise model, and $R$ is a regularizer encoding prior information or desired structure.

This formula is one of the main templates of the course. The data term asks the reconstruction to be faithful to the measurement process. The regularizer asks it to be a plausible image. Different imaging methods can often be compared by asking two questions:

- What is the data term?
- What is the image model or prior?

Classical inverse problems usually choose $R$ explicitly: smoothness, small energy, total variation, sparsity, or another mathematical prior. Neural methods often learn part of this structure from examples. They may learn a direct map from $y$ to $x$, learn a denoiser used inside an algorithm, or learn a regularizer implicitly through training.

The likelihood still matters in learned methods. A neural reconstruction that ignores the measurement model may produce visually convincing images that are not faithful to the data. In imaging, visual plausibility and measurement consistency are not the same thing.

## Data Fidelity As A Contract

A data term is a contract between the reconstruction and the measurement process.

For Gaussian noise, the contract says: large squared residuals are unlikely. For Poisson counts, it says: predicted photon counts should explain the observed counts with the correct signal-dependent uncertainty. For weighted least squares, it says: residuals in reliable measurements matter more than residuals in uncertain measurements.

This contract is only as good as the assumptions behind it. If the noise model is wrong, the data term may over-penalize harmless deviations or under-penalize serious ones. If the forward model is wrong, the reconstruction may fit the mathematical data term while failing the physical problem.

The practical habit is simple:

1. state the forward model;
2. state the noise model;
3. derive or choose the data term;
4. test whether the residuals behave like the model predicts.

The fourth step is often where modeling becomes scientific rather than decorative.

## Reading Residual Images

After reconstructing an image $\hat{x}$, the residual is

$$
r = f(\hat{x})-y.
$$

A residual image is not just an error picture. It is a diagnostic of the model. If the forward model and noise model are reasonable, the residual should look like the kind of noise the model permits.

For Gaussian noise with constant variance, residuals should be roughly centered around zero and should not show strong image structure. If the residual still contains edges, texture, or repeated patterns from the scene, the reconstruction has failed to explain part of the measurement. If the residual is almost exactly zero everywhere, that may sound good, but in noisy data it can also mean the method has fit noise too aggressively.

For Poisson data, residuals are harder to read directly because the variability depends on intensity. A raw residual in a bright region and the same raw residual in a dark region do not have the same statistical meaning. One often looks at normalized residuals or uses the negative log-likelihood itself.

This gives a practical checklist:

| Residual behavior | Possible interpretation |
|---|---|
| structured residual | wrong forward model, underfitting, or missing component |
| residual much smaller than expected noise | possible overfitting to noise |
| residual much larger than expected noise | over-regularization, wrong model, or poor parameter choice |
| residual consistent with noise model | necessary but not sufficient evidence |

The last row is important. A good residual does not prove the reconstruction is true. In an ill-posed problem, several different images can produce statistically acceptable residuals. Residual checks test data consistency, not uniqueness.

## Common Mistakes

A first mistake is to treat all noise as Gaussian because squared error is familiar. Squared error is a model choice. Sometimes it is a good one, sometimes it is only a convenient approximation, and sometimes it is misleading.

A second mistake is to confuse random noise with missing information. Random noise can sometimes be reduced by repeated measurements or averaging. Missing scene content, physical occlusion, or unobserved frequencies require additional assumptions; averaging does not create information that was never measured.

A third mistake is to think that a lower data term always means a better image. With noisy data, fitting the measurements perfectly can mean fitting noise. This is why likelihood must later be balanced with regularization.

A fourth mistake is to call every discrepancy "noise." Some discrepancies are model errors, calibration errors, clipping artifacts, or distribution shifts. A likelihood can only describe the uncertainty it was designed to model.

## Computation

The Week 4 notebook lets you compare Gaussian and Poisson noise, plot signal-to-noise behavior, and inspect likelihood curves.

Run:

```bash
python3 examples/week04_noise_likelihood.py
```

or open the notebook in Colab from the link at the top of this chapter.

When using the notebook, do not only look at the final images. Change the noise parameters and ask whether the same visual artifact would be penalized the same way by each likelihood. Then inspect the likelihood curves and connect their shape to what you see in the image.

## Exercises

1. For $y_i=x_i+\eta_i$ with $\eta_i\sim\mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2)$, compute $\mathbb{E}[y_i\mid x_i]$ and $\mathrm{Var}(y_i\mid x_i)$.
2. For a Poisson pixel with expected count $\lambda=25$, compute the standard deviation and approximate SNR.
3. Explain why Poisson relative noise improves as the photon count increases.
4. Compare the shapes of a Gaussian squared-error cost and a Poisson negative log-likelihood for one pixel.
5. Give one example where averaging measurements reduces noise, and one example where averaging cannot recover missing scene information.
6. In your own words, explain why a neural reconstruction method should still care about the measurement noise model.
7. Derive the weighted least-squares data term for independent Gaussian measurements with variances $\sigma_i^2$.
8. Give one example of random noise and one example of model error in an imaging experiment.

## Takeaways

- Noise models connect physical measurement assumptions to mathematical data terms.
- Gaussian additive noise leads to squared-error fidelity.
- Unequal measurement variances lead naturally to weighted least squares.
- Poisson photon-counting noise has variance equal to its mean.
- Low-light images are difficult because relative photon noise is high.
- Noise and model error are different; likelihoods only describe uncertainty under a chosen model.
- Likelihood terms are only one part of reconstruction; regularization is needed when the inverse problem is ambiguous or unstable.
- Learned imaging methods still need data consistency; plausibility alone is not enough.
